ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Roti Prata or Butter Prata; mapped to the closest parent class flatbread food product (FOODON_03315571) as the most defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Roti Prata or Butter Prata; A0EKL (Flat bread) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept found for Roti Prata or Butter Prata as of 2026.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Butter Prata in Singapore is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 150 g, often served with curry dipping sauce on the side.

nutrient_source.title: Roti Prata (HPB Singapore Food Composition Database) and USDA FoodData Central — Bread, flatbread (SR Legacy 18069) adapted for butter enrichment
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary nutrient values are anchored to HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for plain Roti Prata, adjusted upward for saturated fat, cholesterol, and energy to reflect the generous butter application characteristic of Butter Prata. The input record values (energy 300 kcal, protein 5.67 g, fat 18.67 g, carbohydrate 28.0 g, saturated fat 10.67 g, sodium 300 mg) are consistent with HPB Roti Prata data plus butter enrichment and are retained. Trans fat 0.33 g is plausible from naturally occurring trans fats in dairy butter. USDA SR Legacy used as secondary cross-check for macro plausibility.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Chapati / Roti (wheat flour flatbread, pan-fried)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singaporean Butter Prata in the University of Sydney GI Database. The closest approved analogous food is chapati / roti (wheat flour flatbread, pan-fried) with a GI of approximately 62 (medium). Butter Prata uses the same refined wheat flour base and pan-frying method; the added butter increases fat content which may modestly lower the effective GI, but 62 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI 62 × available carbohydrate per serving (carbohydrate_g 28.0 g × serving 150 g / 100) / 100 = 62 × 42.0 / 100 = 26.04, rounded to 26.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 10.67 g/100 g from butter and ghee exceeds the 5 g/100 g threshold, contributing to arterial stiffness; sodium at 300 mg/100 g is borderline with negligible potassium offset.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Butter Prata to occasional consumption, request reduced ghee and butter application, and pair with a high-potassium curry or dal to partially offset the sodium and saturated fat burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour base with low dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g) produces a medium-to-high glycaemic load of 26.1 per serving; saturated fat 10.67 g/100 g from butter and ghee worsens insulin resistance.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Butter Prata to one piece per meal, pair it with a high-protein, high-fibre curry to blunt the glycaemic response, and request reduced butter and ghee to lower the saturated fat contribution to insulin resistance.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 10.67 g/100 g and trans fat at 0.33 g/100 g from dairy butter significantly elevate LDL cholesterol risk; cholesterol at 33.33 mg/100 g adds further cardiovascular burden.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Butter Prata as an occasional food, strictly limit portion size to one piece, and substitute butter with a MUFA-rich oil such as extra-virgin olive oil to reduce LDL-raising saturated and trans fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 10.67 g/100 g from butter and ghee exceeds the 5 g/100 g RA threshold and promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, fibre >3 g/100 g) are present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise Butter Prata consumption and substitute butter and ghee with extra-virgin olive oil to reduce pro-inflammatory saturated fat and support a lower-inflammation dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Butter Prata is a popular Singaporean Indian flatbread derived from the South Indian and Malaysian roti canai tradition. It is made from a dough of refined wheat flour, water, salt, egg, and a small amount of condensed milk, which is stretched paper-thin, folded repeatedly to create flaky layers, and pan-fried on a hot griddle with ghee or oil until golden and crispy on the outside while remaining soft and chewy inside. After cooking, a generous knob of butter is spread over the hot surface, melting into the layers and imparting a rich, savoury, slightly sweet flavour. Butter Prata is a staple breakfast and supper item at Indian Muslim hawker stalls and mamak restaurants across Singapore, typically served with fish or mutton curry for dipping. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 300 kcal, 5.67 g protein, 18.67 g fat (of which 10.67 g is saturated fat from butter and ghee), 28 g carbohydrate, and 300 mg sodium. The high saturated fat content, refined carbohydrate base, and moderate sodium make it a high-concern food for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis. The glycaemic index is estimated at 62 (medium) based on analogous wheat flour flatbread data, with a glycaemic load of approximately 26 per standard two-piece serving of 150 g. It is halal and vegetarian but contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in butter prata?
What is roti prata made of?
How much fat is in butter prata?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo data for plain Roti Prata adjusted for butter enrichment, and cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy flatbread data. No exact HPB entry for Butter Prata was found; values are adapted from the closest approved source. GI is an analogous estimate from chapati/roti data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Butter Prata is not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA SR Legacy flatbread entries as of 2026. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation is not available in the HPB or USDA sources consulted. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for Butter Prata are not fully reported in the primary approved sources consulted (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central) as of 2026. 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: Butter Prata contains no plant-derived vitamin C sources; confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy flatbread data. 5. trans_fat_g of 0.33 g/100 g is plausible from naturally occurring trans fats in dairy butter (vaccenic acid and conjugated linoleic acid). 6. GI of 62 is an analogous estimate based on chapati/roti (wheat flour flatbread, pan-fried) from the University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021; no direct GI measurement for Singaporean Butter Prata exists in approved sources. 7. The input source listed (ICMR-NIN) was noted but the HPB Singapore Foodo database is the higher-priority approved source for Singaporean dishes and was used as the primary anchor. 8. Ethnic tags 'Curry' and 'Naan' from the input were not carried forward as they are ingredient/dish names rather than ethnic descriptors. 9. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodon_match_type and ontology.foodex2_match_type corrected from 'broader' to 'closest_parent', which is the valid allowed value from the set ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] for a parent-class mapping where no exact term exists.
